And so today the title of the day is all I call Inception Secrets. The reason why I call Inception Secrets is because, first off, I love the movie Inception. I thought it was cool. And I love in the movie when basically when they go inside someone's dream, right, and they're going back and they're trying to place an idea inside somebody's head. Because if they can place that idea inside somebody's mind in their dream and the person thinks it's their thought, thinks it's their idea, then their determination to do whatever that thought was is a thousand times more strong, right? And so I think about that in selling all the time. When we sell stuff online, offline, wherever we're at, if we're selling, there was a day and a time, I don't know when, but in the past when selling was just that was the thing. You either you sell and you close and you do this kind of stuff. And it worked, but it got less and less effective. I think as people were maturing and as the internet and communication changes and just the way we all react, that all of us have more defense mechanisms against selling. And so I believe selling has really transformed and changed a lot over the last five, 10 years, especially just in the last year and a half or so, just with all the stuff that's happening. And so I strongly believe that people love to buy stuff, but they hate being sold to. And so how can you create situations and things and put systems in place where just like inception, we're not really selling something to anybody, but we're creating an environment and an atmosphere and a story and a thing that happens where we cause an epiphany in somebody's mind and also that epiphany lights them up and they think that whatever we're trying to sell them is there I did. For example, this is my failed attempt at inception yesterday. Simon knows the story. So as a lot of you guys know the MMA fights are happening next story yesterday and one of my buddies was like the main attraction and I've been wanting to go to it ever since I found out about it. And I wanted to go really, really bad, but I didn't want to call my wife and be like, hey, you know I've been gone this whole week, doing events late nights, early mornings and having help with the kids. You know one night I have a chance to come over and I think I want to stay here and go to the fights. And so if I was just told that I should have been angry and all these bad things would happen, right? So I'm like, I've got to figure out a way to make this her idea and if it's her idea then she's not going to care, right? And so I've been trying for the last week to orchestrate this thing to cause inception in her mind so that she would have this idea to invite you, like you should go to the fights, right? So that was my end goal. So your end goal should be your customer, like hey, I should buy your product, like alright, that's a great idea. So I've been trying to orchestrate inception on my wife and I think she's immune to my powers after over a decade of being married to me because I was trying thing after thing after thing. Even yesterday she came to lunch and we guys had a chance to meet her and four or five times during my conversation I was talking about Jesse's fight tomorrow and the guy's going to be fighting and how exciting it is for him and the fact that he's in Boise, he never fights in Boise. I kept dropping all these bombs, like just setting it up and setting it up and she never closed the cell for me. And so last night after me and Simon were hanging out, he kept kind of prodding me like you got to make the cell, make the cell. So I called her up and I was like, so what would it take for me to be able to stay and go to Jesse's thing and she's stopped for a second. She's like, I really would like two new chairs in our front room. And I was like, okay, two chairs and if I do that I can stay tonight. She's like, yep, my done deal sold. So we closed the deal. So my inception did not work on my wife. I had to go straight sell with her. But I think she sold me. In fact, if you think about it, she used inception on me. She got her stuff really, really easily. Anyway, but this is the goal of this entire thing we're doing is how do we create these things in people's minds where it becomes their idea? Okay. Do you guys notice the sales process you guys all went through to come here? Do you notice that I never really sold you on it? You were selling me and I'm like, why you should be part of this and why you should be part of it? And we just kind of turned the table and you do that, it changes the whole entire selling experience. Okay. The other reason why I think this is important, and this is really interesting. One of my buddies, John Allinus, told me this a little while ago. He said that they were doing all these studies and stuff. They said that in 1980, the average attention span of a person was 20 minutes long. Okay. And he said that you look back then, like the only distraction that people really had in 1980 is once a day the mailman would show up. Your phone might ring once or twice a day. There's two or three TV channels, so like you flip through and after two or three channels, if it's not there, you're done. And that was about all the distractions we had, right? So the average attention span was 20 minutes a day. And if you look at all of the sales stuff that is taught and people do and people share online, offline, everything, I believe that all the sales that people are using is all based off of people's attention span in 1980. Okay. That's why if you look at all the famous copywriters that are showing that, how to write these like 8,000 page sales letters with 80 word headlines and all this stuff. And I think, and I believe, and based on my testing and results, that stuff did work at a time, but today people's attention is different and does not work nearly as well. And so we have to understand that things are evolving and changing. So anyone have an idea of how long the average attention span is for a human right now today? You're going to be so depressed. He's five minutes here. And you have the good three minutes. You're going to be so unimpressed with us as humans. Think about your kids yourself. How often you're looking for text message and check in Facebook. And that's why we didn't pay for internet access in here. Because I didn't want you guys on the internet the whole time. The average attention span right now, nine seconds. Is that sad? So that's the environment we're selling in right now, you guys. We don't have 20 minutes to try to convince them to buy something. We've got nine seconds. So we understand that it changes how we sell stuff, right? Look at, and I'm going to talk about some of the different sell scripts throughout today, but I look at the people that are doing this really, really well. Not in the how to make money market, the people really well like, think about when you're going through the grocery aisle and you're going to go buy your groceries. What are the things that are created to catch your attention in your nine seconds? You got like the Inquirer magazine, Star, think about those things like that, that they're very fast, short, punchy things that grab you really, really fast. And we'll talk about some of those things throughout. But just the atmosphere is different and we have to understand that. If we're doing sales the way people were doing five years ago, even three years ago, it's not nearly as effective as today because we've got to grab people's attention so much faster. And so because of that, a lot of things have changed. So what I'm going to be sharing with you this morning, the first thing is just more of the conceptual part about how the language patterns we have to use to sell. So this is more of the overall hierarchy strategy of how this whole thing works. And then the next sessions for today are going to be very, very tactical based. I'm going to give you the exact scripts we use at every single piece in the sales funnel. And so we've talked about this whole value ladder concept all throughout the weekend. But we have a couple of sell scripts we use for this part of the value ladder. We've got a couple that we use for this part of the value ladder and a couple we use for this part of the value ladder. So you guys have all those scripts in your book and that's what we're going to be going through after this morning. We're going to go through the rest of those and then you'll have all the pieces of the puzzle. Okay, so that's kind of game plan. All right, so Inception Secrets. In your book, you've got kind of a diagram here that's going to illustrate out all of the core elements I'm going to be talking about during this first presentation. And the very first thing, if you look, this is kind of, I would say the central pivotal part of this whole Inception Secrets is this initial quote right here and I was going to get the guy's name that wrote this. This is not my, I did not invent this. But what he wrote here was really powerful. I think he called it the one sentence persuasion secret or something. It's like a 12 page book that explains this whole concept. And I'll find out if you guys know what it is. But in his little presentation, he said that if you want to get somebody's attention, if you want to sell somebody, these are the five things you have to do for that person. Number one is you have to encourage their dreams. Number two, you have to justify their failures. Number three, you have to allow their fears. Number four, confirm their suspicions and number five is throw rocks at their enemies. If you can do that to your client, they're going to love you. They're going to come to you. They're going to do anything you want. So whenever we're writing copy, we're creating webinars. We're doing any kind of sales stuff. Those are the things we're trying to weave into our presentations. Those are our key elements. Okay, so let me step back a little bit. All right, so the first time that I started thinking about its inception concept, we were working on a big project last year. Some of you guys probably saw it. It was called RIPLIN. And we were working on it with a whole bunch of people. And my job was we were creating all the marketing materials. And we did a great job of it. We got 1.5 million members in 90 days. And then the other guy's job, which was to launch the company, they didn't do a very good job of it. The marketing part was freaking awesome. We did great, right? And we were having these conversations with me and a couple of people that were doing a lot of the marketing materials and stuff. And one of the guys, he kept making these marketing things that were so obvious. So much like this direct selling stuff, right? I just kept selling and selling. I just, I hated it. I hated everything he sent over. I just drove me crazy. And so one day I emailed him. His name's Jonathan. And I wrote him, I said, and I copied this from the email because I just said it one night on my iPhone. I was typing it and I was kind of upset and I sent it. And this is what I said. I said, Jonathan, what I found out in copy is that if you just tell someone something that it's not as strong and doesn't get the emotion needed to cause action. I think that to really make the concept go viral and get people's buy-in, we have to lead them somewhere, but we can't give them the answer. They have to come up with it. We plan in their minds with a story. And if they come up with the answer, then they sold themselves and they will cause the action. Does that make sense? So send that to them real quick. And then like, Temesley, the hero back, he's like, heck yeah, man, it sounds like effin inception. So you guys know Jonathan, he swears every third or fourth word. Anyway, so that's not like effin inception. And that was the first time I was like, you're right, that's what it is. We're, we can't be so obvious. We've got to lead them to a path and then let them make it their idea. And if we do that, everything else changes, right? Okay, so we do that through a lot of, a lot of, a lot of different things. And kind of the core piece of this starts out with these, these three things. The first, I call primary question. Number two is the epiphany. And number three is the magic bullet. All right, and these, this is a progress. I have arrows on the little image there. Okay, so this is, this is kind of the process, the core process of inception we're going to be taking somebody through. All right, and so the first is, is the concept of a primary question. Anyone have any idea where I first heard this concept of primary question? Mike knows, anyone else know? Tony Robbins. So if you go to date with Destiny, Tony spends like an entire, I don't know, probably eight hours on this concept, he calls the primary question. And before, before, before I went to that event, I never even thought about this. We said that every single person in the entire world makes most of their decisions based off of one question that's running in their head all the time. Okay, so we spent this, he did all these exercises to help you figure out what that primary question is inside of your head. And he says that there's typically just one question and it's asked over and over and over and over again. And it dries and fuels everything you're doing. Okay, so for all you guys, I highly recommend for you to sit down and try to think what your question is. But, but he went through and he talked about, for him, his primary question was how, I think it was his, like, how can I make this better? So every situation is, how can I make this better? How can I make this better? And that was his primary question that drove everything. Okay, they talked about other people's primary, excuse me, primary questions. Some people's primary question is what's wrong with me? Okay, do you guys know anybody like that? Every situation they're like, what's wrong with me? What's wrong with me? What's wrong, no matter what happens, what's wrong with me? What's wrong with me? That primary question drives everything they're doing, everything they're doing, every choice, every action, everything they're doing, they have that primary question. Okay, other people's is how can I add value? Other people's are, why am I so scared? Everyone's got this primary question that's running through your head that's making all sorts of decisions for you. Okay, now when you think about it, most, well not most, but all of your consumers, all your customers have these primary questions as well that are driving them. And you really gotta figure out what that question is, because if I wanna take somebody somewhere, I gotta figure out what's the question they keep asking in their head. Now, Tony, when he was teaching, he was talking very specifically to everyone internally, what's the primary question you have about yourself? And he wanted you to be able to become very aware of your primary question, because when you knew it, then you can start, it's just like this marketing. So when you become aware of it, you can manipulate it, right? He has become aware of the funnels and why we do stuff when you become aware of the preframes and all this stuff. When you become aware of those things, now you can manipulate them and change them, right? So Tony was showing us, because as soon as you become aware of your primary question, now you can change it and do something that's gonna serve you as opposed to holding you back, okay? And so, for my purpose with this more is to understand that, again, people have a primary question, but I also think that inside of each of your markets, when somebody is making buying decisions, I think they have primary questions as well, okay? About your product, your service, whatever it might be. And so for our business, the very first time I really realized this was when we first launched our call center and we were doing all this stuff and prior to this launching a call center, I always have support people. So there's kind of a barrier between me and my customers. I don't always hear all the feedback that's coming through from them. And so, which is unfortunate, I think that me and everybody should spend a little more time looking at your customer support, because this is how you start finding out what your primary question is. Like what things do people keep asking over and over again? And so one day, we launched a call center, we had, I don't know, any 10 sales guys or something. And one day I went in, I just wanted to hang out and just hear these guys sell. Like I really enjoy kind of energy in the buzz of a sales room, right? So I go in there and I sit down, I'm just listening. And within probably an hour or so, I started hearing the same recurring thing over and over and over again. The majority of the people they were talking to, kind of in the business opportunity space, what they kept asking every single time was, well, this works so good, why don't you just let me have it for free and I'll pay you guys from the profit I make? That was their question every single time, well, this works so good, why don't you just give it to me for free? If you're guaranteed to make me money, just give it to me for free and then I'll pay you for whatever I make from it. Okay, and sales guys had to always spend that over and over and over again. But that was like the primary question that the business opportunity market kept asking over and over and over again. And I had this, I could pit me like, wow, that's what they want, that's their core question. So how do I, instead of doing what most people do and get upset and just be like, cuz you're moron, you can't do that. That's what most of our things is like, cuz you have to give me money. But I was like, instead of me just getting angry, how do I use that? How do I use that as a tool to get these people to do what I want them to do, right? And so a little while later, we were launching one of our offers. I'm gonna read you guys the first couple lines to this because you'll see how I kind of use that as a tool. All right, this is what our dot com secrets FX offer. So I've got a quick question for you. Have you ever wanted to work from home, own your own business? Come on, you know you want that lifestyle, the one that everybody talks about where you can work at home in your underwear or on a beach with your laptop. So why hasn't happened for you yet? Come on, admit it. This isn't the first time you've been looking for a proven way to make money, is it? When is it your turn? My guess is this, you saw someone online promising you riches and told, but there was a catch. Doesn't seem like there's always a catch. You pay hundreds or thousands of dollars to get started, right? Before you make any money. Don't they know that's why you're there because you need money? Doesn't seem like if all those gurus, courses and software work so good and they really believe in them, why don't they just give you their money making system and then after you've made your money, then they would get paid. I'm speaking to exactly what they're, they're like, yeah, that's how I feel. Okay, I'm coming right where they're at and boom. And all of a sudden, that's exactly how I feel. And so then the copy from there goes on and basically spins like, look, I'm not like these other gurus, I care about you. I want you to be successful. So this is what I'm gonna do. I'm gonna let you come in for free, you have 30 days to come in for free. And for 30 days, I'm gonna coach you, I'm gonna teach you, I'm gonna give you everything I have. And then at 30 days, you will be making money. If you're not making money, don't pay me anything. Okay? Cuz that's, that's the deal. But if you are making money, then just pay me part of the profits. Okay, I only charge 97 bucks a month for this membership site. So in 30 days, if you're not making at least 97 bucks a month, cancel. If you are, do nothing, we'll bill you 97 bucks a month and I'll keep training, keep being your coach. Okay? So that was the message. You guys think that's pretty strong? Okay, we launched that within 45 days, we had added over 8,000 members, that membership site on our dollar trial, which then rebuilt 30 days later at 97 bucks a month. Okay, the right message got in their head, understood the primary question and grabbed them. Okay, so for you guys, what's the primary question that most of the people are asking? Okay? You're gonna make money market, you probably know now. If you're in the weight loss market, what is it? Okay? If you're in the finance market, what is it? If you're a coach, whatever it is that you're doing, what's that question they're asking? Cuz if you can figure out what that question is, instead of hiding from, like everybody else is doing, you embrace that, and somehow use that to take your offer where you want it to be, you'll be amazed how people will just follow you and start running with you. We heard like 8,000 members in a membership site in 45 days. Yeah, that's awesome. Okay, so that's the first thing. Now, after you understand this primary question, the next step is we've gotta lead them somewhere. We've gotta lead them to some kind of epiphany, all right? And this is, I learned this concept initially from Dagan Smith. And Dagan's one of my best friends online, he's awesome. And he has this whole like story selling course and process he takes people through. And when I was going through the very first time, he was talking about this kind of concept of telling story, how you capture somebody, and there's elements of what he taught me that are gonna be in some of these other scripts you guys will see, cuz we use a lot of it. One thing he says is that the entire purpose of a story, excuse me, is to grab somebody, tell them your back story, okay? It's the whole tract of care, so tell them your back story, bring them through a process, and you have to explain to them how you had an epiphany, okay? And the goal of you explaining how you had an epiphany is cuz you want them to have the exact same epiphany that you just had, okay? If I just tell someone like, I want to add an epiphany, I need to build a list. They didn't, they're like, okay, that was a cool epiphany. The reason why we use story, and why story is so important, cuz I need to take them on that journey with me, cuz I take them on that journey with me, and I tell the story about how I figured out I needed to build a list. Somewhere in this story, they're gonna have the exact same epiphany I did, usually a few seconds before I actually tell them what it is. And if I do that, what just happened? It was their idea, not mine, right? So for example, in our product that were teach people how to build a list, I go through this, my story, I think I kind of share a lot of the story with you guys on the first day. Basically I tell a story about, hey, I was reading this article about this guy that was talking about how all these gurus make $100,000 sending emails, and I didn't believe it was true. But he told me this is how it works, and so I wanted to figure out how it works. I went and I bought this email list of a million people, and I loaded it up. Remember that story I told you yesterday, I tell this whole story, and it bombed, and it didn't work, and all these gurus are scam artists, and then I went to school, and I snuck out, and I logged in to my PayPal account, and I made sales. So it actually worked, but I had to figure out the right way to do it. But I kind of tell this whole story, and I lead them down this path, to all of a sudden they're like, man, I need a list. If I just had a list, everything would be good. Just like Russell, that's a Russell's whole problem, but he made money. If I needed the right way to have a list, then I could make money. And all of a sudden, I'm like, hey, by the way, guys, I'm gonna teach you guys the right way to build a list. I give them that epiphany, they have that epiphany, that's theirs. It's their idea, they figured this thing out. Inception was just caused. At that point, I'm like, hey, I got a product that's gonna show you guys exactly what we just talked about. You guys see that? So that's the epiphany portion of this process. So we're leading with primary question, we're transitioning that question into the big epiphany I need to give them. The epiphany is gonna bring them to the core concept I'm teaching them. Now, this is one of the keys, you guys. It's really important to understand. I've mentioned it every single day since I think we've been here. In every sales message you have, there needs to be one thing, okay? The one core thing. I promise you, if I was to critique all your copy, the biggest problem all you guys are gonna have is you've got two or three or four things. Okay, Perry Belcher taught me this, and I believe it 100% to be true. He said that in every sales letter he'd ever written, if he's trying to get somebody to buy into one concept, it converts awesome. As soon as he makes and tries to buy into two concepts, conversion drops. Three concepts, it drops, four concepts, it keeps dropping. Okay, I look back a lot of my old webinars from even a year ago, two years ago, I'd always be like, okay guys, I teach you about this and this. And since I did the and in the middle, conversion's dropped. Okay, my epiphany all needs to be tied into the one thing. The one thing. I look at the reason why the dot com secrets excels video did so well. Cuz the only thing, the only epiphany I was trying to get these people to have is the fact that they needed to have a list, that was it. Okay, and before I was gonna teach them this and this and this and this, okay? When we started doing the high ticket secrets webinar that we did last month, I struggled, I kept creating this webinar over and over and over again. I was like, had three or four core things I wanted to make sure everybody understood. What's why I came back and said, no, the one thing, what's the one thing of everything that's most important? I cut everything out, I had just one message for the entire webinar. I had the one thing, it just crushed it, okay? So you gotta figure out that there's one thing in each of your cells message, do not try to sell them on two concepts. You not only have to get them to buy into one belief, and if you get them to buy into two, it's gonna drop your conversion. So figure out what, again, every cell's process, whatever years you're doing, what's the one most important thing, and that epiphany is gonna lead them back to that one core thing, okay? Yep, it does not matter if your product is two things. That's a great question. Even if your product is two things, do you only tell them one? A good example of that. So in Dotcom Secrets Labs, one of our upsells is our automated webinar course, which is two, three events we did like this, so six days worth of events. The first time I tried to sell it, I was like, this is like the most amazing product in the world. So I'm like, you're gonna get this, you're gonna get this, you're gonna get this, you're gonna get this, cuz there's so much stuff. And I spent like 20 minutes explaining all the stuff they're gonna get. I'm like, man, people are gonna be blown away by all they get. Conversion sucked. I could not ever get to work. We tried, I rewrote that cells that are four or five times, never get to work. And then one night, this is the first, actually the first time I had the epiphany of the one thing. I was sitting there and I was like, of all the stuff that I'm sharing, like what's the most exciting thing? And I looked back an entire six days worth of the course. And remember in that course, I spent about 15 minutes teaching people about a concept called the stack, which by the way, you're gonna learn probably after lunch. I taught this concept about the stack. And I was like, that's, of everything in here, that's probably the one sexiest part. And so I took our cells video, I deleted the whole thing, I started over from scratch. And the entire video talks about the stack. I learned this thing from this guy named Armin and I tell the results. This is what happened with it. And the first time I did this, the second time I did this, and I just walked through this process of the stack and how much money it ended up making me, and how good it was, and how I've used it, and every single thing I've ever done. And just that one thing changed my entire business. And literally, that's a 15 minute segment in a six day, six days worth of a course. That was the only thing I talked about. And then when I did the pitch, I talked about that, I said, and also you're gonna get a bunch of other stuff that's really, really awesome, that's gonna teach you a bunch of other things. But man, the stack alone is worth this, and you're gonna get that, plus everything else for this one price. And that was why I'm finally converted. It's been converting like crazy ever since. So even if you have this huge thing, you gotta pick the one sex thing and that's what you're speaking to. Okay? Okay, do you guys hear that? So his question, and this is one of the guys, every single copywriter in the world. I promise you, I've had this argument late nights with all these great copywriters, and I think they're all wrong. Okay? Every copywriter will teach you, you need like 80 pages of bullet points on every single thing you do. The only bullet points I've ever written is for .com secrets labs, and that's just cuz I was trying to show off. Okay? I've had nothing to do with me wanting to sell stuff. I don't think, the old school thought is, let's try to sell this, we're gonna have tons and tons of bullet points because we have no idea which thing is gonna be the most sexy to somebody. So let's give them every single thing, and then hopefully one of these bullet points will convert that person to a cell. Okay, it's old school thinking. That's thinking I've got 20 minutes attention span. I'm looking at nine seconds, so I don't have the luxury of giving them everything. And I thought I was crazy, there's actually one dude who agrees with me, and the guy happens to run the only billion dollar information company in the world. So I feel kind of good about it. So I was literally, I was, I had this idea and I've been arguing with all these great copywriters about it. And then one day I got this book called Great Leads by Michael Masterson. Anyone ever read that book or seen it? You see, it's awesome books. I'm reading the very first chapter of the book and I'm on a flight flying back east somewhere. And I'm sitting there playing, I read this. And I'm seriously freaking out. So I'm like, I'm right. I'm right and everyone's playing asleep. I got nobody to tell that I'm right to and I'm freaking out. Right, I'm like, I can't tell my wife cuz she doesn't care. But I'm like, this is the cool thing. So this is what he said in the book, okay? Again, this is Michael Masterson, not just me. So I'm leveraging his credibility, but I think he's right. He said most copywriters conjure up a list of features and benefits and create ads that mention as many of them as possible. The thinking behind this approach goes something like this. I wonder which of these benefits will really push the buttons I want. I don't know. I guess what I'll do is throw them all in the promotion. That way if one doesn't work, maybe another will. This is what we call toss salad approach advertising copy. Throw everything on the counter into a big wooden bowl, marinate with some connecting sentences and toss. It's standard recipe for B level copywriter, but it's not the way to win any blue ribbons in the most competitive kitchen of breakthrough advertising. Pretty awesome? I was right. Again, I think there's a time and a place for bowl points. I said on our doc.com series labs, we did it. But as a whole, we got nine seconds you guys. So if you have nine seconds to pitch something, you gotta pick the best possible thing in the world. That's what you gotta be focusing on. Yep, that's a good question. So the one sex thing you bring out is it vary with the temperature of the audience. Yeah, I would say it probably would. I've never looked at that deep, but yeah, because in all honesty, the primary question is gonna change with the temperature of the audience as well. That's why if you look at the image in the book here, right before primary question down here, the arrow, we come back here to the temperature stuff, right? We got most aware product wear, solution wear problem, and unknown, hot, warm, cold. So you're right. Because somebody who just wants to make money, okay? There are probably questions way different than somebody who's got an existing business and wants to take the next level. Someone who's 300 pounds overweight and someone who's in great shape and they want to take the next level of different primary questions. And so I think for a lot of you guys, you gotta figure out who's the real person you want to sell to the most and start there. And again, that comes back to where those fish at, are they in warm traffic, cold, those type of things. Yep, so what she's asking is different people are motivated by different things, okay? What you're kind of leading to and what you're saying is 100% true. So everyone in the world is motivated by one of two things. Either they like to move towards pleasure or away from pain, okay? Have you guys ever studied NLP before, Neurologistics Program? This is like one of the core things. People make all their decisions either between towards pleasure or away from pain, okay? It's important to understand that. Now, what I mean by that is towards pleasure is in the make money mark is I want to be rich, I want to have a plane, I want to do whatever, right? Away from pain is I hate my boss, I want to quit my job, I'm miserable, I want to get away from this pain, okay? Now, every single one of you guys, every one of your customers all make their decisions based on one of those two things, okay? And I'm curious real quick, think about yourself for a second. I want to know, all of you guys in this room, do you make the core amount of your decisions moving towards pleasure or away from pain, okay? So who here makes their decisions moving towards pleasure? Okay, who makes it moving away from pain? Okay, now this is gonna be a big a-ha hopefully for some of you guys. Typically, entrepreneurs make their decisions moving towards pleasure. Which is why 90% of women here makes their decisions moving towards pleasure. Okay, now the mistake that most of us make is we assume that our customers are the same way and so we write our messaging moving towards pleasure. But the vast majority of the human population, I would say 90% of people on earth make all their decisions moving away from pain. Is that a-ha for any of you guys? So we come in because we're entrepreneurs and we know this will motivate us, let's sell this way as much as we can. But we're not our customers. We're hyperactive entrepreneurs, you can't sleep next, we're so excited to work the next morning. Okay, we want that pleasure, we want that stuff, most of people just want to get out of pain. And so a lot of times just switching your messaging towards moving people away from pain is way better. Who had a question? Joey? Yeah. There's not a right or wrong answer. Everyone's wired one way or the other. So it's understanding your customers where they wired and not where you're wired. You're always stuck up to like, well, this is how, what, I would have bought that, doesn't matter what you would have bought. Okay? I was listening to Ben Settle podcast on the way over. He's got this podcast about marketing, all he ever talks about is picking up chicks, it's really funny. And he was reading some personal out of some girl row and he's just ripping on the girl and it was really kind of funny. And he's like, she doesn't understand that all that meant, he's like, this is what men actually want. He's like, the biggest thing that you have to understand is you can't sell what people aren't buying. You have to sell what people are buying. That's a big key here, right? So people are buying, getting away from paying and we're trying to sell make, hell there's a bunch of pleasure. They're not gonna buy that, you gotta sell the right thing. Okay? There's another question somewhere? Yeah, David? The other interesting thing about this is you know, like our goal as entrepreneurs is always to like, under promise and over deliver. We focus on one thing, they come in for that one thing. And all of a sudden you got the rest of your thing and they're like, holy crap, I can't even tell you about this other stuff. This is all this free stuff, right? And they get blown away. So it's twofold. One, increase the front end conversion because you're focusing on one thing. Boom. And number two, they love you twice as much because what they're getting in the back is just so much more. All right. Cool, cool, all right, let's keep going. Okay, so next step then, after we focus on primary question, moving towards the epiphany, then the last thing is when you make your offer, again, as much as close to a magic bowl as possible. The other thing about like, is this a magic bowl? Is this gonna solve, is this gonna answer the primary question and solve the epiphany that they just had? What's the magic bowl? And if we can give them a magic bullet, it's awesome. Which is the reason why I like free trials, the reason why I like the invisible funnel, which is the reason why I like free plus shipping because this is a magic bowl, like I just solve all these problems, boom, I'll give you this thing for free, right? I'm scratching that it's really, really easily at a low ticket. So for some examples of magic bolts we've had tons of success with. The dot com secret sex is only pay after you make money, that's our magic bowl offer. Our double your reading, pay $100 after you've doubled your reading. Living on a coupon, don't pay until you've saved $20 your next shopping trip. Girlfriend in a week, don't pay until after you get a girlfriend, right? Like the magic bullet type offer, I love those for the front end of anything, okay? So when I'm looking at this stuff, you guys, let's put that up there. I'm looking at that, that's the core thing here, right? Primary question, moving to epiphany, moving magic bowl, which all this is kind of the one thing, okay? So that's kind of the overall hierarchy of how I'm doing all my cells messages, right? So from a high level, I'm trying to take them, everybody through this kind of a thing. Now with this, we can use this hierarchy in a ton of different situations, right? Video cells, letters, automated webinars, webinars, phone calls, everything else cells I'm doing, I'm doing this kind of stuff. But this is the overarching hierarchy of kind of where we're going with it, right? Now inside of that, there's a couple of the really powerful things that I found that have had a huge impact. And these are all these things down here. Copy speed, trial, close, and desire, and fire. So I'm gonna kind of go through those right now. So the first one is copy speed. So I've hired a lot of copywriters in the past, I had copyrights work full time for me, I've written a lot of copy. And I always did things the exact same way that all the copywriter guys teach, right? I bought all the copywriting courses and all the things people were teaching that worked back in the 80s, back when people had 20 minute attention span. I believed that I did it and we had success with it. Not like screaming success, well, we had good success, but it was kind of like that's what it was. I remember one day, when my friend came to me, he's like, hey, I've got this offer, it's doing really good, you should promote it, okay? And so I'm like, all right, I'm gonna try it out. I send an email out to my list to promote this product. And it was weird because typically when we're promoting something as an affiliate, we're looking to find out kind of what our EPC is. EPC stands for earnings per click. So typically, in the how to make money world, when I send an email out, most people want to make $1, maybe $2 per click. If you make $2 per click, that's a pretty good campaign. So if I send 3,000 clicks, I'm gonna make $6,000 back, right? A really good offer might have $3 per click. So I send 3,000 clicks and make $9,000 back in, right? So that's kind of the metric we're always looking at. And so I send just a test email out to this guy's offer. And the very first email I sent out made $10 per click. Okay, so I think I sent 3,000 clicks and I made $30,000 back in. I was like, holy crap. So I was like, why did that work so well? I could figure out, and I didn't send a bunch more emails because it was working so good. And what was interesting is, and this is kind of the power of having an offer that converts. If you get an offer that makes you $3 or $4 or $5 a click, you literally can become a millionaire in like five seconds. So this guy launched this offer, had no product launch, no anything, and within eight months made $10 million selling this offer. Cuz it converted. Okay, so that's kind of why we do all this stuff we're talking about. You get something that converts, game over. People will come to you begging you to promote it all day long. Cuz all he did, he emailed me and said, hey, so and so got, you know, whatever $6 clicks on this tryout, I sent out, got $10 clicks. And next person just went wildfire, right? And everyone's talking about it. So I went back to them and I'm like, why did this, why did it do, I've seen it three times more than anything I've ever seen ever in the past. I started looking at it and I looked at the sales letter and it was structured so much differently than I'd ever seen anything else before. It still looked like a long form sales letter, but I looked at the sentence structure and there was never any line that had more than one line. There's never a paragraph that had two lines or three. And every single sentence ended with dot, dot, dot. I was like, this is weird. And I started looking at it and I just, I read this entire thing and I was like, the style was different than anything I'd ever seen before. And so I emailed the guy that his offer, I said, who wrote the sales letter for this, it's just different, like who is that? So he told me the guy's name and so I called the guy up and I just wanted to get to know him and he's like, tell me, like, this is just different, like some difference happening and it's working, so I want to know what in the world is happening. And so I don't think he even knew, I don't think this day he knows why, but it was working, so I started asking him and I was like, let me see other stuff you did. So he showed me four or five other pages he had written and all of them had the same style. Like where did you make that up from or where did you learn that? He's like, I didn't learn, I just, that's just how I write, that's just how it works. And so I'm like, all right, so I started from a couple of these other ones just to see as well and same thing, it was like $5 EPCs, $6 EPCs, I just crazed numbers on every single one of the things he wrote. And I was like, there's something new here, something completely different. And so I started kind of reverse engineering his stuff and started seeing why it was working and then from there, the next two or three cells that I wrote, I started following the style. It was very short sentences, a lot of dot, dots, dots after them. And it's completely changed how I write. This style, I can write a cells that would have taken me anywhere from 10 to 14 days. I can write in usually three or four hours now. It shrunk my time down and the conversions go up every single time. So I'm gonna show you guys kind of examples so you can understand what I'm talking about, how it looks. Cuz the part of its look and the part of its kind of the speed of how it all goes together. Okay, so this is my cells that are for dot com secrets x. So you notice every single one of these sentences is like five or six words. They almost all seem like a headline. The speed of this is really, really fast. Let me read it to you. I got a quick question for you. If you ever wanted to work from home, own your own business, come on. You know you want that lifestyle, the one that everyone talks about, where you can work from home in your underwear or on a beach with laptop. So why doesn't it happen for you yet? Come on, admit it. This isn't the first time you're looking for a proven way to make money, is it? So how fast that is to read? Okay, again, we got nine seconds, right? People are scanning stuff down. You gotta block a paragraph, you go like this and you stop, you're like, ugh. And you get an email an hour or something, send you a paragraph and you're like, ugh, I'll just read it later and you just ignore it, right? And you never come back to it ever, okay? We got nine seconds. So every single of these things is almost like a headline and it's grabbing you. And notice how unfinal it sounds. Like everything's like I'm throwing up these things in the air and these dot, dot, dots keep me coming back and keep me coming back. It's like, yeah, it's never ending and it starts pulling me through this letter, right? That's why I call it the speed of the copy because it's like, in piano they call it the staccato, the fast. That's why I feel like there's this fast, fast, fast, fast, fast speed of the thing. And so when I write any of my letters now, all of them are like this, okay? There's never more. Once in a while it might drop to the second line, that's a very rarity, very, very rare to ever let it do that. Very short, very fast, and it sucks people in and it works. And if you look at this copy, that whole thing, you notice, I'll scroll down, you'll see it. That was the first time it dropped to the second line and I was like freaking out, gosh, I didn't make a whole other line for that or should I keep going? See how easy it is to read? If you saw that, you'd be like, I can read it real quick. A lot of space, a lot of open stuff. So I'll walk you through, and I'll walk you through the scripts later to show you the process, but just this as far as the style for speed and ease and everything that, it's just awesome. You'll get addicted to this way, you'll start writing everything. You ever get an email from me or text? My wife's like, why do you always put dot, dot, dot at the end of every text? I'm like, I don't know, I just have to. Yeah, I agree a million percent. Email, every communication with anybody now is this. Emails will go out, videos, sales letters, everything is in this fast, decado, high-paste, easy to write. When you start writing this way, you'll see how much easier it actually is. It's really weird, I can go so much faster and tell a story. Again, you guys see the scripts, Marty, you'll see the things, and you'll write four or five of these little lines for the first thing, the next thing, the next thing. Within a couple hours, your entire thing's done, and it's beautiful, and it's in your voice, and it's awesome. Do you have some? So the way I write now, so I script my whole thing out like this, and then I make, if I'm doing a video version, this is just the same video. So each PowerPoint slide is just one little sentence. Next one, next one, next one. The exact same thing. The worse you're spelling, the worse your grammar, the better. Yeah, the second you, like, if your English teacher reads it and is proud of you at all, you just failed. It needs to sound like I'm talking you in a conversation. In fact, for a lot of guys, the way they do it is they get out, like, something, and they record themselves saying it all, and then transcribe it, because this has to read like we're talking. If it reads like you're reading, it ruins the whole thing. So I use a lot of weird words. I use gunna's and unhorrible grammar and dots, and I want it to visually look like, look easy to read, and I want it to sound like I'm having a conversation with them. So the way I would talk to you if we're talking face to face is exactly the same way like this. If you read this, you're like, that sounds like Russell's talking. That's the way he talks all day long anyway. I want to sound, I want it to be in your voice. Okay? Yeah, I get your English teacher's are going to hate you. Yes. And I promise you at least once a week, when you start driving traffic, somebody who will email you like, hey, I saw your sales letter. I corrected all the errors for you. Here's a new version. Like literally, we get those a couple times a month. I'm like, thanks. I'm not going to do anything about it. And they're like, I said, you just like three weeks, why haven't you updated it yet? I don't care. But I promise you there are people on your list or who come here who are so obsessed with English, and they're like, feels so bad for you. They're like, let me fix this, and just don't worry about it. Just again, it's speed, it's tempo, it's pace. That's what we're looking for. Okay, so the next thing, so Brent and I, we have a project called Overcome Pornography. It's a product that helps people overcome pornography addiction. And like, two or three hours later, I'd written this like huge entire sales letter, and we're like, holy crap, that turned out awesome. And this literally happened to be a flight. But do you guys see how some of these elements I'm talking about, like, do you feel the speed of that? Like you're sucked in and you're just like, oh, oh, and it keeps pulling you, pulling you, pulling you. And it's not like, oh, here's a big paragraph I got to read through. And then what's the next? It's just, it's fast, it's speed. Anyway, so that's one of the big parts of this whole inception thing, how we have to sell today is that speed, right? We went from 20 minutes to nine seconds, and so we have to address that. We can't keep saying what all these marketers are saying. I'm like, oh, well, good copy wins all. In some markets, it does nurse cells or our supplement. We're targeting people that are like 60 to 80 years old. That market, we have a long form cells letter, a bunch of paragraphs, and old people like that still have attention spans. They don't have cell phones, and so it works for them. Other markets, majority of the markets, doesn't work, right? If your market is texting and Facebooking 85,000 times a minute, you need to be working like this. You got to be speeding up everything you're doing, speeding up your copy. All right? Okay. All right, next one is trial closes. So this is a concept that was like earth shaking to me when I first figured it out. I was at a seminar, and this was probably two or three years ago. I was at the seminar, and there's a whole bunch of really good speakers there. It was like a public speaking seminar, so they had all these really good speakers in the room. I got up in the stage and I started speaking, and all of a sudden in the back room, I see this little guy walk down and sit down. I knew who it was. It was a guy named Ted Thomas. Have you ever seen Ted speak before? They call him the Pied Piper, because when he's done speaking, he literally, I've seen videos of him at like Robert Kiyosaki's event. He'll be walking like this, walking like this. He's a line of people all behind him, all holding their credit cards in their hand, and they're walking like this. You have 300 or 400 people falling to the back of the room and buying a sing. It's like the coolest thing in the world. And so he's just down and I'm like, oh man, there's like the man that closes everybody here watching me pitch. I get a little nervous. I'm doing my thing, and I sold. I did well. I got some skills, so I did all right, and people ran back and they bought, and then I go back and do my thing, and I'm taking orders and doing the whole dog and pony show. And he's kind of hanging out inside during this thing I keep seeing, and I'm like, oh man, this is going to be awkward. Whatever's going to happen next is not going to be good for me. So everyone leaves, and it's lunch time, and he's like, hey Russell, I love your presentation. I love to take you to lunch. I'm like, all right, that'd be cool. So we go to lunch, and we start talking, and he's just asking me all these little questions, and just kind of probing me about whatever, and I'm answering him, and I'm kind of shaking my head, and I'm going through some stuff, and then he stops and he said, look at your head. I'm like, what? Look at your head right now. Yeah, he's like, you notice how you're nodding? I'm like, yeah, he's like, that's what you're doing wrong. I'm like, what do you mean? He's like, when I speak in an audience, like if you sit in the back of the room and you watch my presentation, you'll see a wave of heads going like this, the entire 90 minutes. And he's like, when you do that, at the end, everybody will buy. I'm like, well, how do you do that? He's like, the same way I was doing it to you. He's like, what have I been saying for the last five minutes to you as we've been talking here at lunch? I was like, you're just asking me little questions. He's like, notice every single question was a yes or no question? Yeah, he's like, you know, the answer to every single question was yes. I'm like, yeah. He's like, notice that you nodded your head every single time I said that. I'm like, yeah. He's like, that's the secret. He's like, when I sit in the back of the room, he's like, nobody's head's removing. Everybody's sitting there like this. He's like, you did well because you've got some talent. You've got some stuff because you need to get people saying yes from the very first second you get on stage and then you'll be saying yes the entire thing. He says, the way I do this is through a concept I call trial closes. Does that make sense? Okay, does that make sense as a trial close? Yes. Yes, okay. Yes, yes. He starts, he starts like, here's a bunch of trial closes I use and said, he said, the way that I first figured this out is I would, I would, not that I figured out, but I would do this and get people to say yes and I figured out that this was something, right? In NLP, they always talk about like getting someone into yes states. It was like, from the first second I get up, I ask a question and then I do something. I get something, I'm saying something. In my entire presentation, I'm saying yeses. He said, the first was really hard for me. He says, what I did is I took a bunch of note cards and write out these different trial closes like, does this make sense? And I put it on the floor and I speak and another one like, are you guys getting this? And I put it down and then, isn't that awesome? And then, all these different trial closes and he would lay them all over the stage and when he walked around, every time he stepped on when he looked down and he said, does that make sense? You walk over here, are you guys getting this? And he would do that until he became part of his pattern, right? He did over and over and over again the point where he had these all in his head and then he would just drop these trial closes all the time throughout. I was like, that's pretty cool. And so he gave me this sheet that had like five or six pages of all these trial closes he had done and so I took that home and at the time we just launched our automated webinar for dot com secrets local and I was like, I want to add these trial closes in. And so, I basically, I re-listed the entire like two and a half hour webinar and every single time I had a little timeline I'd say, okay, three minutes and 22 seconds I should insert this trial close and I wrote it down. And then at this time I tried this trial close and I went through the entire thing and I listed out like five pages of trial closes. In fact, if you look at the dot com secrets labs book in the very, very back, I show you everything I did here. So I show you all my trial closes. This is like my thing. So at one minute and five seconds I added this and at 121 I added this trial close, 207 I added this trial close, 305. So everything throughout, it's every trial close I added back into that webinar. So I just recorded it and had my brother, he was in my video, so I just spliced those little trial closes in throughout the whole thing. And then we put the, and a couple of things about trial closes. I got so blatant like the very beginning, because I'm in a webinar instead of like at home so I'm like, I gotta get people's nod and their heads like crazy. So one of my very first trial closes was like, it was almost like a joke we put it in and I was like, I know I can't see you guys at home right now, but I want everyone of you guys to shake your head yes if you can hear me. Like literally I need you to shake your head yes. So like I had people like, I just told them flat out like, just do that and I was really blatant with it. A couple of other really cool things is like, that Ted taught me, so the biggest problem is a lot of times you'll share a testimonial and then you get a little nervous and you move on to the next topic. He said, as soon as you share a testimonial, that is the best, most important time for you to drop a trial close or two or three or four. So I just showed a testimonial assignment. I'm like, here's a assignment he's made of bajillion dollars, isn't that awesome? I just did, so that was a trial close. So I showed that, I'm like, isn't that awesome? Can you guys imagine yourselves doing that? Would that change your life right now if you were to have that same result in your life? What would your spouse think about you if you did that? Would that be awesome? Okay, just drop like five right there in a row. So anytime you do a testimony or you do that, anytime you share a really cool feature or benefit, like this is the cool thing that this does. You guys get that? You see how awesome that is? Is that one of the coolest things you've ever seen in your life? You see how this would change the future of your life forever. Okay, boom, trial, trial, trial, trial, trial, close. So I did that in this webinar, went back through, I think I have the numbers in here. We started testing the EPCs. So before we added trial closes, we were averaging, oh please say the numbers in here. Anyway I think we were averaging, I have the after number. Oh yeah, so when we first did it we were averaging $9.45 for every person who registered for the webinar. After we added in these trial closes, driving the same traffic, we increased it to, oh come on, can I not find it right now? It went up $74.6, it went from like $9 and whatever, $0.46 to like $16 per person. So 74% increase just by adding in these little trial closes. That cool? You guys getting this? Is that awesome? And so I started realizing that this was powerful and I started using it first off in my personal life. I use this all the time. I think it drives my wife crazy but it's just the way I speak now, right? It's become part of my language pattern. In my sales videos I use it all the time. In my webinars, in my every time I communicate with any audience I'm using it because I want people to say yes. Natasha will you share your story about this? Because it was like, was it two years ago or when was it that we first talked about this? I think it was maybe even like a year ago or a little over a year ago. I heard Russell talking about this and I was like nodding my head. I was like, oh my gosh, this is awesome. I've got to do this. But then I was like nervous. I was like, how am I going to ever remember these things? And then I remembered what he taught about the guy that had flashcards on the stage. And so I had a webinar and so I decided that I was going to put it all on post-it notes. So I had something like 50 trial closes on post-it notes all over my window, all over my desk, and Rich walks in and he's like, what in the world is going on? And I'm just like, you know, they're falling all over the place. I'm like, after the webinar, which it worked out really well, I don't have the numbers, but I made a lot of sales. And I remember that night I walked out of the office and I got a trial closed up to my shoe. So now I always talk in trial closes, always. My new one is, are you getting this? Would this work for you? Could this work for you? Can you see yourself doing this? Once you start practicing, it's really, really weird to do it at first and you feel completely stupid. But then all of a sudden it just totally becomes a part of your speech pattern. Does that make sense? Y'all getting that? All right. Will this work for you? Awesome. Can you tell me also what you said about one of your attendees at your event was like, I feel like you actually, that was awesome. Oh yeah, yeah. So I was in a group coaching call and one of our premium buyers said, the one thing I love about Natasha, she always says, are you getting this? Does this make sense? And she's like, it just shows that she cares. She wants us to really get this. And it's awesome. And she's like bought everything that I've ever offered for sale. So yeah. So yeah, no, it definitely, it just makes people think that you, you know, and you should care, you know, but if they said, you know, I'm not getting this, you know, I don't know that I would necessarily have to change everything. But yeah, it does. Now the last thing is desire amplifiers. And there's a lot of desire amplifiers we can use, that we can use throughout. And so I'm going to share just a couple of different types because I think that they're important. One of them that's kind of cool, I heard a guy, he's a good copywriter. I heard him speak on stage one time. And he shared this little nugget. It was really, really big for me. He was talking about copywriting in the Bible or something. And one of the desire amplifiers he was really focusing on was the seven deadly sins. Who here knows what the seven deadly sins are? Who can, here they are all the same. So you've got lust, gluttony, greed, sloth, wrath, envy, and pride. Okay. Now usually in our mind we think the seven deadly sins is this really bad horrible thing, right? As salespeople we need to realize that those are the things that get people to take action, all right? So when you think about that, when you're writing your copy you've got to think like, how can I make somebody lust after this? Because they're going to lust after they're going to want it. How do we get them gluttony? How do we use gluttony as a tool, as an amplifier to get somebody by my product? How do we use greed? Okay, how do we use sloth? So greed obviously make money, make money, make money. Sloth, do it without any extra work. Just sit on your butt at home and you're doing nothing, right? Wrath, envy, pride. You're not going to use all these in every single thing you're doing, but think about like, they're powerful, they're powerful things, right? So how can you weave one or two or three of these in your messages? Because they're amplifiers. They're things that all of us have inherently. They're problems we have and we can tap into those. I don't want to sound like an evil person, but we want to tap into those kind of things, right? Okay, the next thing, yeah, I'll show you the things that's kind of cool. I don't want to, hopefully I'll do this justice. So this is a concept I learned from Tony Robbins and it had a huge impact on me and had a huge impact on how I sell people now. He has a concept that he teaches called the six human needs. Have you ever heard about this before, you guys? I'm sure a lot of you guys have thought about this from one aspect, but not from like a selling aspect. So one thing Tony says is every single person, we do things for different reasons, but there's only six reasons why we do anything. We understand that you realize that everybody does everything based on one of six things. It gives you a lot of ability. It's all, so you become aware of this, you can manipulate it, right? So there are four needs of the body. There's four needs of the body and then there's two spiritual needs. Okay, so I'm going to go through these. So the four needs of the body, the first one, let's see, I'll do one, two, three, four. Okay, the first one here is certainty. Every single one of us in this world, we need certainty. We need to know that when we wake up in the morning, things are going to be certain. We crave that, we desire it, it's a need we have to have in our lives. The next thing we all need is we need variety. We want something new, we want something exciting, we need variety. Okay, something interesting, do you notice that certainty and variety are opposites? Certities, I want to know everything's going to be perfect. Variety, I want craziness, I want all sorts of fun and excitement. Okay, this is like the enigma of humans. Okay, these are opposites. We need them both. Okay, next thing that all humans need is we want to feel significant. Okay, I'm very special, I feel significant, I feel great. Okay, we all want significance. It's an important part. It's part of all our identities. The other thing we all want is we want love and connection. Love and connection. Okay, I want to feel love, I just want that love. Now, significance and love and connection are opposites. Significance is look how great I am. Love and connection is difference, love, right? But we both need these, but they're opposites. Okay, and then over here on the spiritual ones, we have growth and contribution. Wait, the growth and contribution one or is the growth one and contributions one? Growth and contribution. Okay, growth and contribution. Okay, now humans don't have to get these needs met. We can do without these and still function, but every single one of us will get these needs met. And we will give up our beliefs, our values, we'll give up everything to make sure those needs are met. Strange. Now, the other cool thing is that any situation in life where three of these four things are met, it causes an addiction. Okay, so for example, the first time I went on a date with my wife, I thought she was beautiful, went out with her and we were talking and everything I said she smiled and I got a lot of significance from her, but she thought it was pretty cool. And we were out on a fun date, we did as much stuff, got a lot of variety, it was exciting and that night she gave me a hug and a kiss and I felt love and connection. Boom, instant addiction. Okay, that's what we all like falling in love. Okay, it meets at least three of our needs and we fall in love. Now what happens a year later we get married, a year later we have kids, we're at home all the time and all of a sudden things kind of are the same every day, variety goes away, but we have certainty. Every morning we wake up, we know she's there. So we got this addiction. What happens after a while when your wife stops thinking you're not cool? Right? Has that ever happened to any of the men in the room? And all of a sudden you don't feel significant anymore. And what happens when you have kids and then you have another kid and another kid, suddenly your wife loves these kids, she doesn't remember you anymore. Well I got certainty being met, the other ones aren't being met very well, so what do we do? We have to have these needs met. Okay, so what happens? What happens to most men and most women or whoever? We start seeking these needs somewhere else, right? For some people they go and they find a business. In a business they show up every day, they got a business they're building to get certainty. Every day it's this new thing, they get variety, okay? Their customers love them, they get testimonials, they get love and connection. Their team, their boss, people think they're awesome and they get significance and all of a sudden their job fulfills all their needs. And what happens? It's been 10, 20 hours a day at work because that's what their needs are being met. Interesting? It causes an addiction. Who here is addicted to work? Because your needs are being met, okay? It's interesting and so all these things are happening and one thing Tony said that was interesting, he said that there's never a situation where anyone's ever gotten divorced, where in that relationship all their needs are being met. Okay, if you want to like save your marriage, if you're struggling with marriage, look back and say how am I making my wife feel certain? How am I lighting her up? How am I making her feel significant? How am I getting love and connection? If those needs are met for her, she will never leave you. It's either her guy, if your wife's fulfilling those needs for you, you will never leave. But the second they start disappearing, as humans we have to get those needs met and we'll find out a way to do it. Okay, think about if you have kids, think about your kids in school, okay? If they're not getting these things from home, where are they going to turn? They're going to turn to sports, they're going to turn to drugs, they're going to turn to gangs, they're going to find somewhere to get these needs met, okay? And that's how it works. So this is the motivational, personal development part of it. Now that you're aware of it, how do we use this for making money? Okay? How can we create situations where these needs are being met with our customers? We can cause an addiction in our customers where they're feeling certainty, they're getting variety, they're feeling significance, they're getting love and connection. We can literally cause an addiction with them where they're going to want to keep buying from us, come into our vents, come into our webinars, they're going to get all these needs met with us. And what else is cool is that, is that moving on, like, to really take your life to the next level is then you start speaking after the spiritual needs. When these needs are all met, then you start looking for how can I grow? How can I contribute, right? So same thing. How can you take your customers to the next level where now we're growth, we're contributing, okay? We took one of our customers to Kenya with us for a week and we built schools out there with them. We had a whole contest to get. We wanted to help our community to grow and to contribute and be part of something bigger than they were, okay? So these six human needs, like I said, there's a lot of principles you guys can use personally in your own lives, in your kids' lives. Well, now with my kids, I'm going through this process right now because I know that right now my kids get all their needs met from us, right? It happens. When they get older, it's going to shift and if I don't focus on it, all sorts of crazy stuff can happen. But if I know that their needs are met here, they're not going to go to other places, you can engineer that, right? They're not going to be seeking for other places, okay? We've got to, anyway, so it's a cool tool you can use in all aspects of your life but also with your customers. And excuse me, it's not so much the point where I'm trying to hypnotize you but I kind of am. One thing I can do with hypnosis is I can attempt to cause amnesia. Okay? So what I mean by that is, is if a customer's coming in and they're having experience with you, right? And they've been buying a lot of things. They got burned by all the other bad evil gurus in the past. I have language patterns I can use to help them forget those things so they focus on me. Okay? And they're subtle and they don't sound like a big thing and you may think that's not going to work but it really does. Like for example, I might end my sales video or my webinar back. Look, I'm sure that all you guys have been in this internet marketing space for a long time. You've probably seen the scam arts. I've seen people trying to rip you off, trying to take your money but for the next 90 minutes I need you to forget about all that stuff and put it in the past. Okay? For the next 90 minutes I need you to focus on what we're talking about today. I need you to have hope and faith because what I'm going to share with you is going to work. Okay? That's the hypnotic statement. When I'm taking this thing and I'm helping, I'm telling you, I'm causing amnesia so you'll forget about all the bad things you are because one of the reasons why you're not going to buy it for me is because you've been burned by somebody else in the past. So if I can use hypnosis to try to take, to try to put the light behind you so we can focus on moving forward, it works. Okay? Causing amnesia. The next thing is telling them what to remember. So a lot of times I do this in a webinar like, look, you're going to watch this webinar right now but what's interesting is tonight you're going to be laying in bed and for an hour, two hours before you fall asleep, the thoughts in your mind are going to go over and over again, what if I did this, what if I did this, what if I did this? And as those thoughts go through your mind you're going to know that you have to take action and you have to be part of this. Okay? I'm telling these things and placing some subconscious thoughts in their minds and that tonight a lot of people are going to sit there and be like, it's going to go through their head. A human mind is really weird how it works. When you believe in hypnosis or not, I can place thoughts, I can place ideas in your head and it'll do these really weird things. Okay? So tell them what to remember. Dissociation. Dissociation is where I try to take somebody into a different place. For example, you'll notice when we go through the scripts, a lot of times I try to future pace people. I talk about something that like before we move on, I want you to really sit back and think about what life's going to be like in 30 days from now. You've got this stuff like what's it look like to you? What's the future going to look like? And I kind of, I push that to them like future pacing. I'm just associated from the situation they're in right now where they're miserable and I'm showing them this, where they can become, where they can go. It's a really, really powerful thing. I think most of the scripts I have usually at least once or twice from some kind of future pacing, we're breaking away from where they are and pushing up to where they want to become and where we're trying to take them. They can see that for just a second. Very, very powerful tool. Another one that I like doing is time disortation. Anyone here have been hypnotized on stage before? Kind of a fun process, right? And I remember that entire experience, we were on stage for like 2 or 3 hours and it felt like it went really, really fast because we're in this kind of hypnotic state. And so for me, a lot of times I'm trying to sell somebody, especially higher ticket stuff, we're taking them through a longer process. A webinar, our Dotcom Secrets local webinar, I think is 3 hours long right now. It's a long time to sit on a webinar, listen to Russell pitch you on something, right? But I need somebody to do that. So I'm going to feel like we're here for less than an hour, but because of that you're going to feel so much better. And every, you know, blah, blah, blah, something like that. Does that make sense though? So I'm telling them like, this may be long, but you're going to feel way shorter and you're going to feel like this is fast paced, blah, blah, blah, and you do that and it places that thing in their mind. Another one that's really cool, Howard Berg taught me this, he does it a lot. Because he knows that like when you start teaching people, they're going to get overwhelmed. And so he does it at the very beginning, he puts people in a hypnotic state where he says, no, as you start going through this training, initially you're going to be overwhelmed. That's a great thing. And as you become more and more overwhelmed, that's okay. That's a good feeling. And that overwhelmed feeling means that you're learning, you're progressing, blah, blah, blah, blah, whatever. And he said that when he started doing that, his refunds of his course dropped in half. Because suddenly he was telling people that like that feeling overwhelmed was a good thing and kind of changed the state of it where typically people are like, I'm overwhelmed, give my money back. So this is, I talked about this a little bit on day number one, like concept of immersion. So this is what I like to do. Like I'm, I recommend this for all of you guys. Like I remember, first time I kind of started realizing the power of this, it, it was huge. Like I literally think that in about a 48 hour period of time, I can become an expert in any business. I can know it was much in 48 hours as any expert. I literally think that if I want to become a doctor, I can spend 48 hours and do almost everything a doctor can do. It gets really, like when you start learning how to learn fast, like we learn all this crap in school. It takes us, you know, entire semester to cover a bunch of stuff. But like the reality is, if you learn through immersion, you can learn like 98% of what you need to do in very, very short, finite period of time. So what I will do, a lot of times I'll pick a topic that I'm really passionate about. Like when I first wanted to learn NLP, I was like, hey, I want to learn NLP. I can go and read a book on it and do some stuff in six months and I know a couple of things or I can immerse myself in it. So I literally went online, I bought every single NLP book, every single audio, as much stuff as I could and I got it all shipped to me and I spent a week where I'm going to learn NLP. I'm going to learn as much as I can from it. And so in the car, I'm listening to NLP CDs and books. I'm reading through them, scanning. I'm trying to find as much stuff and I just immerse myself in something as, like as hard as I can throughout a week. In a week, you learn, like you literally can learn enough in a week on any topic, you can become an expert in it. Okay? Again, and so that's kind of what I do. So like when I got into NLP, that's what I did. I was strolling around, I had no stuff about five or six sales, had no books, some audio courses and podcasts, just brained them as fast as I could. And I don't know everything on it. I don't finish reading every single book. My goal is not to read from cover to cover, it's to scan through and find the things I can use that are applicable to me as fast as I can. And when you do that, you just, you just simulate information really really fast. And what else is cool is like, when I was talking to Howard Berge, the world's fastest reader, he told me something that was really powerful. He said, he said a lot of people read a book on a topic and they get like one author's perspective. And so the problem is that that's just one guy's perspective. He's like, I'd rather get five or six books and get everybody's perspective and also you get a whole picture. And obviously most of us can't read an entire book in three minutes like Howard can, so it's harder. But I like to get a bunch of different stuff and get a lot of perspectives on stuff because you get a better picture and you start making these like, these connections that you don't make when you dabble, right? Like the reason why I think college is horrible experience is because I spent an hour here and then an hour here and I learned like eight topics in like, you know, a three hour period of time and then three days later I do it again and again, I'm dabbling and all these things. I can't remember anything and none of these connections are happening. But I think one topic and just cram as hard as I can for a very finite period of time, you get so much stuff. That's why I like events like this. And three days I cram so much stuff on you. A lot of it's going to be over your head. You can get overwhelmed, all this stuff. But all of a sudden all these connections start getting made and you're like, oh, that's how this stuff all fits together. But if we do this same experience over three months, most of you guys would never be able to get all the connections. We're here, I think, the majority of you guys will at least get the connections. Then you can go back and start filling the pieces for what's important by doing stuff like that.
